text: The surgeon begins a hand-sewn colocolonic anastomosis by suturing the posterior wall at the midline, aligning the distal and proximal bowel ends. Using V-lock sutures, the surgeon runs a full-thickness suture from lumen to lumen, ensuring the suture is cinched down adequately without excessive tightness to avoid a tourniquet effect.

The surgeon continues suturing the anastomosis by advancing stitches intraluminally to extraluminally on the distal limb and extraluminally to intraluminally on the proximal limb, using a fenestrated bipolar instrument for guidance. The anterior canal is formed with running U-stitches, followed by the initiation of a second posterior suture line using a 3-0 V-lock and V-twenty needle, overlapping the initial suture line for reinforcement.

The surgeon transitions to the canal suture, taking full thickness bites from out to in and in to out, while carefully rolling the corners. The final canal stitch is completed, and the V-lock sutures are tied together. Running interrupted Lembert sutures are placed to reinforce the suture line, particularly at the intersecting point. An endoscopic view shows the anastomosis, with insufflation confirming no leaks.
